title: Message loss and the impossibility of certainty
description: Over an unreliable network no finite exchange guarantees both sides agree; protocols cope with probabilistic acknowledgement, retries, and idempotency, not certainty.
body: Use when reasoning about why delivery and agreement are never absolute. The Two Generals result shows no fixed number of acknowledgements guarantees mutual agreement over a lossy channel, and FLP shows no deterministic protocol guarantees consensus in a fully asynchronous system with even one crash; real systems sidestep this with timeouts, randomization, and retries. Do not promise exactly-once delivery or guaranteed agreement in bounded time. Actions: design for at-least-once delivery plus idempotency and use timeouts to make progress in practice. Validation signals include an idempotency test under duplicated and dropped messages. Risk boundary: a protocol that assumes reliable delivery breaks under loss; require explicit retry/dedup handling before trusting any cross-node acknowledgement.
